intro: A five-minute guided tour of the Todo service. Follow the lessons top to bottom; each one is short and ends in an "aha".

topics.title: Big Picture
topics.summary: How a request flows through the app

lessons.title: Three layers, one direction
lessons.body:
The whole service is just three layers stacked in a line: **API** (HTTP handlers) â†’ **Domain** (validation & types) â†’ **Storage** (the database).

A request only ever flows *down* the stack, and data flows back *up*. No layer reaches around another â€” the API never touches the database directly, it always goes through Storage.
lessons.aha: If you can name which of the three layers a file lives in, you already know 80% of what it's allowed to do.

lessons.title: Follow one request end to end
lessons.body:
`POST /todos` lands in `createTodo` (API). It hands the raw body to `validateTodoInput` (Domain). If that passes, it calls `insert` (Storage), which writes a row and returns the saved todo. The handler serializes that to JSON with a `201`.

Every write path in the app is a variation of this same three-step dance.
lessons.aha: "Validate, then persist, then serialize" is the shape of every mutation â€” once you see it once, the rest of the handlers read themselves.

topics.title: The Domain Layer
topics.summary: Why validation lives in its own layer

lessons.title: Trust nothing at the edge
lessons.body: `validateTodoInput` takes an *untrusted* object and returns either a clean value or a list of field errors. The handler never trusts the request body â€” it trusts the thing validation hands back.
lessons.aha: The boundary between "untrusted input" and "trusted data" is a single function call. Everything after it is safe to use.

lessons.title: Type guards make the compiler your ally
lessons.body:
`isStatus` is a *type guard*: a function that returns a boolean, but whose signature (`x is TodoStatus`) tells TypeScript to narrow the type when it returns true.

That's why the status field can be a plain string coming in, but a strict union everywhere downstream â€” the guard is the one place the narrowing happens, and the compiler enforces it for free after that.
lessons.aha: A type guard is runtime validation and compile-time proof at the same time â€” one small function buys you safety in both worlds.

topics.title: The Storage Layer
topics.summary: How the app talks to the database safely

lessons.title: One tiny function guards every query
lessons.body: Every read and write in the app funnels through `query` in `db.ts`. It grabs a client from the pool, runs a **parameterized** statement, and always releases the client in a `finally` block.
lessons.aha: Because there's exactly one door to the database, SQL-injection safety and connection cleanup are solved once â€” not re-argued in every repository method.

lessons.title: The repository speaks Todos, not SQL
lessons.body: `insert` and `list` in `todo-repo.ts` are the only functions that know the table's column names. They take and return `Todo` objects; the rest of the app never sees a raw row.
lessons.aha: Change the schema and only the repository changes â€” the shape of that blast radius is the whole point of having a storage layer.
